text.content:
Phase 7 — The Seventh Question

Alternate title: “The Road That Was Not Sailed.” BR2 Phase 7, v0.7 draft. Tier 2/early Tier 3, levels 8–11. The phase where the party turns a phrase into a place.

What this phase is

Phase 7 is not the descent. Phase 7 is the phase where the party translates the Seventh Stair clue, triangulates the route, survives countermeasures, and locates the surface threshold. By the end, they know where the Seventh Stair begins on the surface — but they have not yet entered the true deep route. That is Phase 8.

The conspiracy is no longer only trying to hide documents. It is trying to hide geography. Phase 7 should feel as if the enemy has shifted from clerks and couriers to surveyors, mapmakers, and the people who decide which roads appear on which charts.

What Phase 7 delivers

  • “Seventh Stair” is a route marker connected to old descents, mining roads, or sealed underways — not a port term, not a metaphor.
  • “No sail. No road.” means the next transfer bypasses normal surface travel.
  • The Ashen Itinerary, Iren's analysis, Aldea's material observations, and old route records can be combined to locate the surface approach.
  • The surface threshold is in or near the Cairn Hills, hidden behind an abandoned mine-road, an old cairn, a sealed tollhouse, and a collapsed survey station.
  • A concrete destination for Phase 8: the Seven-Cut Cairn.
  • Any captured NPC (Haskur, Kethren, Korre, or others) can assist, mislead, bargain, or become a liability.
  • The enemy begins actively destroying route evidence — not just hiding it.

What Phase 7 must NOT deliver

  • The full conspiracy.
  • The identity of “E.”
  • The names Eclavdra, House Eilserv, Vault, Underdark, Demonweb, Lolth.
  • Confirmation that the “E.” writer is non-human.
  • A full below-ground dungeon (that is Phase 8 onward).
  • A single-NPC or single-check solution.

Player-facing language constraint

Do not use the word Underdark in read-aloud, handouts, or NPC dialogue. Use instead: below, deep road, descent, underway, old stair, permitted descent, sealed route, route beneath the roads.

Tone

Scholarly, tense, investigative, road-haunted, old-stone eerie. The political and administrative horror remains, but Phase 7 shifts from ledgers and couriers toward maps, old route glossaries, mining permissions, sealed waymarks, missing surveyors, and roads that stopped appearing on official charts. The conspiracy did not just hide in institutions. It hid in old infrastructure no one remembers how to name.

Phase flow

  1. Phase 6 aftermath. Sort evidence and prisoners. Branching opening based on what survived the Old Stonecistern.
  2. Consult interpretive sources. Iren, Aldea, Vone, Caelith, Dalmor Vetch.
  3. Build the triangulation. Five evidence categories: Name, Function, Permission, Material, Place.
  4. Enemy countermeasures. Route-cleansing. False maps. Witness pressure.
  5. Field reconnaissance. Northeast out of Greyhawk, into the southern Cairn Hills.
  6. The Seven-Cut Cairn. Discovery, not entry.
  7. Phase 8 threshold hook. The descent begins next.

GM summary (one paragraph)

Phase 7 is about turning a phrase into a place. The party leaves Greyhawk City with a piece of unsettling language and arrives at a piece of geography that ought not to exist. Every source they consult gives them a fragment; only triangulation produces a destination. Meanwhile, the enemy is doing the same work in reverse — defacing waymarks, altering ledgers, replacing maps, frightening surveyors. The phase ends with the party at a ruined Cairn Hills tollhouse, at the foot of an ancient stone marker cut with seven descending lines beneath a black hill, looking at the place where a road would begin if a road were what came next.

Opening Read-Aloud

Use after the Old Stonecistern, regardless of which ending Phase 6 produced. Adjust the second paragraph to match what the party is actually carrying (a prisoner, ash, both, or only memory).

The morning after the cistern, Greyhawk goes about its business as if nothing has changed. A baker on Tannerway is shouting at her apprentice. Three barge-hands are arguing over a pulley line at the High Quay. A toll-clerk at the West Gate yawns and waves a wagon through without looking. The city's indifference is its weather; it does not turn for what happened underneath last night.

You have paper ash on your hands, or wet stone, or a prisoner with bandages, or only the memory of someone slipping through an iron grate. You have a handful of phrases that mean less than they should. No sail. No road. Seventh Stair. Andren transferred. You have a sketch of seven cuts beneath a black hill that does not match any chart, port-book, or county map you have ever seen.

Someone — it might be a half-Suel manuscript dealer in the Garden Quarter, or a retired sergeant at the Hand of Coals, or a courier you carry yourself, or only your own voice — says, quietly: “This is no longer about who paid Haskur.”

“It is about where obedience goes when it leaves the surface.”

GM note

This read-aloud is the pivot between Phase 6 and Phase 7. The investigation has changed shape. The party is no longer following a man; they are following a permission. Let the table sit with the phrase “where obedience goes when it leaves the surface” for a beat before opening the next scene.

Suggested transition

After the read-aloud, present the party with the appropriate Branching Opening (A–G) for what survived their Phase 6 climax. Then open with the first interpretive node they choose — almost always Iren Velash, but Aldea, Vone, or a captured NPC are all valid entry points.

Opening A — Haskur Captured

Read aloud

Haskur Vandrell sits at a small table in a borrowed back room, his coat over the chair, his wrists shackled at the front in a way the party has chosen not to make worse than necessary. He has had a bad night. His face is the colour of unwaxed paper. He has eaten nothing and asked for nothing except an opportunity to talk first.

When you sit down opposite him, he looks at the door, then at the window, then at the lamp, and finally at the lead PC.

“You keep asking who paid,” he says. “That was never the question.”

What Haskur is

Frightened, bitter, calculating. He is alive because the party kept him so, and he is aware of that. He knows the phrase Seventh Stair, but he was never meant to know the location. He has been told only where to stand at the handoff. His usefulness is real but limited.

What Haskur will say

Use these as Haskur's tier-one reveals. He gives them in order with light pressure (Insight DC 12, Persuasion DC 13). Each additional tier requires either threat, hard-won leverage, or a genuine offer of survival.

Tier 1 — under light pressure

  • “The boy knew the route. Korre knew the papers. I knew only where to stand.”
  • “Seventh Stair is a handoff word. Not a place to me.”
  • “The route was older than Sereth's people. They were renting it, not running it.”

Tier 2 — under harder pressure (Insight 14, Persuasion 15, or a survival offer)

  • “Korre called it an account. The boy called it a permission.”
  • “I was to be admitted. I was not to be told.”
  • “The marker is real. I never saw it. The boy described it once: seven cuts under a hill that wasn't a hill.”

Tier 3 — only if Haskur is offered protective custody, exile, or a witness's name (Persuasion 17, real promise)

  • “If the route is in the Yeomanry, I would not know it. If it is east, I would not know it. Korre's ledger said northeast.”
  • “Whoever opens the marker must be expected. Otherwise the marker is empty stone.”
  • “You are looking for a road. Stop looking for a road.”

How Haskur misleads

If the party threatens him without offering survival, Haskur points them toward a false stair: a dry quarry shaft west of Greyhawk, six cuts visible on a stone, no seventh. He will not admit the deception until he is offered something to live for. Use this to seed the False Stair node (Phase 7 Nodes — Node 6) if the party is treating him too roughly.

Evidence categories from Haskur

Name (confirmed), Permission (confirmed). He cannot supply Place or Material directly.

Interrogation mechanics

ApproachDCYields
Insight (read his fear)12Tier 1 lines — he wants to be useful.
Persuasion (offer survival)13–17Tier 2 or 3 lines.
Intimidation (threat alone)15Tier 1 only, then false leads.
Arcana (detect compulsion)15He is not under compulsion. He is just afraid.

If Haskur is left under guard too long

The Prisoner/Heat Clock advances. By the third day, a forged custody order arrives in Greyhawk. By the fifth, a quiet extraction attempt is made. See Phase 7 — Clocks & Countermeasures.

Opening B — Haskur Killed

Read aloud

Haskur Vandrell's body lies on a low table in a borrowed cellar. Someone has folded his coat and laid it across the rail by the door, the lining turned out. There is a single brass button in the lining's seam that was not there when he was clothed. There is dust on his boots that did not come from any street in Greyhawk.

The first frost of the season has finally come. By daylight, the city will know there was a death at the cistern. By tomorrow, the death will have a price attached to it, and someone will want to be paid.

Inventory of H. V. Andren's effects

Hand the party Handout 1B — Inventory of H. V. Andren's Effects (see Phase 7 — Handouts). Significant items:

  • Worn Aerdy travel coat, lining tampered with — one brass button hides a folded slip with a single name and date: “V. K. — 8th of Patchett.”
  • A stained traveler's belt with a tiny silver crescent buckled into the lining (Aerdy commercial-house token, defaced).
  • A leather purse: 38 gp, 12 sp, two pewter trade-chits stamped “Tarnsmere Toll — route account.”
  • A small canvas pouch of stone dust. Not quarry-stone. Not river-stone. Heavy, slightly oily. Iron-grey with a thread of green. (Aldea can read this.)
  • A folded paper in a hidden pocket of the coat. Half-burned at the edge. Reads only: “… no sail. no road. VII. cairn under the …”
  • A small brass crow figurine, hollow. Inside: a cipher slip with three letters: T · S · C.

How the story continues

The story continues through documents, witnesses, and material. Haskur's death increases Greyhawk Heat by 1 segment automatically and makes Kethren or Korre the more valuable living thread. If both are also unavailable, the party works from documents alone — viable but slower.

Evidence categories from Haskur's effects

Place (Tarnsmere Toll chits, “cairn” fragment), Material (the stone dust pouch). The cipher slip T · S · C resolves later as Tarnsmere Spur Cairn.

Greyhawk consequences

Heat Clock starts at +1 from Phase 6. By the third day, the Watch will ask routine questions. Avoid public possession of the body or visible mourning; the party should arrange a quiet disposal or hand the body to a sympathetic advocate (Halask, if Vector D survived).

Triangulation note

Haskur's effects deliver Place + Material (two categories). Combined with any one of Iren, Aldea, or Vone, the party reaches 3+ and can begin field search.

pages.name: Opening C — Haskur Escaped, Evidence Recovered

text.content:
Opening C — Haskur Escaped, Evidence Recovered

The default successful Phase 6 ending. The party lost the man and gained the route.

Read aloud

Iren Velash's shop opens its shutters at the second bell of morning, half an hour later than she advertises. The reading table has been cleared. The bound manuscript at the bench has been moved. The brass lamp has been polished overnight. On the table where she normally pins her commissions, the Ashen Itinerary lies under a sheet of clean blotting linen, four corners weighted with smooth river-stones the size of plums.

She gestures you in without greeting. She locks the door behind you. She does not put on the kettle.

“Sit,” she says. “Do not lean on the table. I have been reading since the third bell.”

What Iren has determined overnight

Read into Phase 7 — Primary Nodes — Node 1: Iren Velash. The arrival opening covers the moment of handoff; the substance of her analysis lives in the node page.

What the party brought

  • The Ashen Itinerary (intact or partially burned).
  • The route mark (rubbing or sketch).
  • A wax flake (recovered from Korre's apartment or the cistern).
  • Any of Caelith's road notes, if useful.
  • Possibly Doras Kemmel's testimony (if Doras turned).

Triangulation note

Iren is poised to deliver Name, Function, and partial Place from a thorough reading. That alone is 3 categories — the party can begin field search this same day if they wish, though they will likely also consult Aldea (Material) and the Guild (Place).

pages.name: Opening D — Haskur Escaped, Evidence Mostly Burned

text.content:
Opening D — Haskur Escaped, Evidence Mostly Burned

The fail-forward ending. The party is working from scraps, but the campaign continues.

Read aloud

The morning is colder than the day before. You have spread on Iren's reading table what you actually salvaged from the cistern: a scorched corner of foreign paper, a single wax flake the size of a fingernail, a sketch one of you made by lamplight of the route mark scratched into the platform wall. It is not much.

Iren does not say it is not much. She rearranges the items twice, looks at the wax flake under a glass for a long time, and then says:

“We have less than we should. We have more than nothing. Stop apologising and tell me what you saw.”

What the party has

  • A scorched fragment of the Itinerary with “no sail, no road” and VII Stair still legible.
  • One wax flake from the cistern platform or alley.
  • The route-mark sketch.
  • Tellan Verth's amended statement (Phase 6 Handout 2), still valid.

Effects on Phase 7

  • The Route-Cleansing Clock starts at 1. The enemy has a head start because the party is moving more slowly.
  • Iren can deliver Name and partial Function, but not full Place — that will require the Guild and Aldea.
  • Field searches require 4 of 5 evidence categories instead of 3 to land at the right site (the cumulative head start makes 3 categories produce only the right region, not the cairn itself).

The encouragement

Do not apologise to the table for this opening. The investigation has produced enough evidence to find a stair if the party works carefully. Phase 7 is built to fail forward at every node. Increase the dread — the enemy is one step ahead — but never close the door.

Opening E — Kethren Captured

Use in combination with A, B, C, or D. Kethren is rarely the sole survivor of the Phase 6 climax; usually he is a captured supplement to one of the other outcomes.

Read aloud

The young man in grey-and-bone clothing sits very still in the room you have given him. He has not been bound; he is not the sort of prisoner who responds to bindings. Two PCs are in the room at all times by rotation. The door is locked from outside.

He has not eaten what you brought him. He has drunk only water. He has not slept where you can see him sleep.

When you ask him his name, he says, “Kethren.” When you ask if it is his name, he says, “It is the name I use.”

When you ask him about the Seventh Stair, he says nothing for a long while. Then he says, “I will answer questions that are questions. I will not answer questions that are speeches.”

How Kethren answers

Literally. Narrowly. Always technically true. He will not monologue. He will answer direct, single-clause questions if pressed. He will not name “E.” He will not name the deeper organisation. He will not name the species, the city, or the destination beyond the Stair.

Sample exchanges

Q. What is the Seventh Stair?

A. A permitted descent.

Q. Who permits it?

A. Those who hold the gate.

Q. Who are they?

A. Not surface people.

Q. Where is it?

A. Where the surface roads end at the marker.

Q. What does the marker look like?

A. Seven cuts. Beneath a hill that is not a hill.

Q. Where is the hill?

A. Northeast.

Q. Northeast of what?

A. Of where we are sitting.

What Kethren reveals (evidence categories)

Function (confirmed: descent), Permission (confirmed: it is admitted, not entered), and partial Place (northeast, seven cuts, hill-not-hill).

What Kethren will not say

  • “E.”'s name or species.
  • The destination below the Stair.
  • The deeper organisation.
  • Why Blooming Rot began.

If magically compelled

His answers are technically true and still maddeningly narrow. Zone of Truth works on him — he simply will not lie. He will also not volunteer information. The party must ask the right questions. If the questions are wrong, the answers will be useless.

If detect thoughts or similar mind-probing magic is used

The surface of his mind is route diagrams, ledger pages, and a single dim space he is consciously avoiding looking at. He knows he is being read and will look at literally anything except the destination. A skilled mage may catch the word “recall” flickering with fear. He is more afraid of being recalled than of being killed. Insight DC 16 reads this even without magic.

His ask

If treated humanely for a week, he will once — quietly, without theatre — say: “Do not return me. Whatever you do, do not return me.” Use this as the campaign hook it is.

Opening F — V. Korre Captured

Use as a supplement to A, B, or D. Korre is often the most valuable Phase 6 capture — she knows the surface apparatus better than anyone else the party has touched.

Read aloud

Veyra Korre sits in the chair you set out for her with the air of a woman who has been at exactly this table before, only in a different city, with a different cup, and a different set of strangers across from her. She has not eaten. She has not asked for anything. She has been waiting for the negotiation to begin.

When you sit down, she says, “Before you begin, I want to know three things. One: am I being charged with anything that would put me in a Greyhawk court? Two: am I being held on Yeomanry authority? Three: who is willing to sign for my disappearance?”

She lifts her water cup, drinks once, and sets it down without sound. “If you can answer two of those three, we can talk.”

Korre's offer

Korre is not loyal to the conspiracy. She has been disciplined about not asking what she carries, and she is now finished with that discipline. She knows where route evidence was stored, who handled it, and what must be destroyed. She wants:

  • Legal protection in writing, or
  • A managed disappearance into Aerdy (her preferred), or
  • A reason to believe that her surviving correspondents will not be punished.

If the party can offer any one of those, she will tell them what she knows.

What Korre knows

  • Seven-Cut appears in her older ledger as a route account, not a place. She has never visited it.
  • Tarnsmere Toll is the relay name — it appears in one altered ledger line and one packet receipt.
  • The route is administered by a body that does not have a Greyhawk presence. She receives instructions, never persons.
  • She knows the wax recipe-family Aldea cannot place. She does not know what it is, only that it has been the same wax for the eight months of her current account.
  • The Hand of Coals' barkeep and a porter at the Customs House both received small payments from her account over the same eight months. The conspiracy paid for silence in the smallest visible places.

What Korre does not know

“E.”'s identity. The species of her principal. What lies below the Stair. The deeper organisation. The full purpose of Blooming Rot.

Evidence categories from Korre

Place (Seven-Cut, Tarnsmere Toll), Permission (route account vs road), Name (confirmed). She cannot supply Material directly but can describe the wax pattern in enough detail that Aldea or Iren can confirm.

Useful Korre line

“My jurisdiction ended at the marker. I have never been the kind of woman who steps past the end of her jurisdiction.”

Opening G — Everyone Escaped

Kethren, Haskur, and Korre are all loose. The party has only documents, witnesses, and material. This opening is not a punishment — it is a sharper, faster phase that demands cleverer use of the surface NPCs.

Read aloud

You have woken to a city that does not know what happened to it. The cistern is sealed by a Watch officer who is asking the wrong questions to the wrong people. Greyhawk's morning bells are ordinary. The Brass Crow's shutters are closed; a small handwritten sign on the door reads “Under reorganisation.” Kestrel & Reed is open and refusing all unfamiliar custom. Veyra Korre's apartment over the bakery is being scrubbed by two strangers with strong arms and quick hands.

You have a route mark, a glossary entry, a witness statement, and a wax flake. You have no prisoner.

You have until the enemy finishes erasing the surface.

Why this is still winnable

The party has at minimum five places to go: Iren, Aldea (by courier or in person), Vone (if he survived Phase 6), Kestrel & Reed (if they can be approached without being warned away), and the Guild of Miners and Jewelers. Combined with Tellan Verth's amended statement and the route mark itself, the party can hit 3–4 evidence categories within two in-game days.

Effects on Phase 7

  • Route-Cleansing Clock starts at 2. Korre's address has been stripped. Kestrel & Reed has been warned.
  • The Greyhawk Heat Clock from Phase 6 carries forward without modification.
  • Iren, Aldea, and Vone are all reachable but skittish. Dalmor Vetch is still available.
  • The False Stair node (Phase 7 Nodes — Node 6) is automatically seeded by the enemy this phase: a planted false map will reach the party within the first two days unless they refuse all unsolicited information.

Tone note

Make this opening feel hungry. The party is not behind; they are racing. Reward speed. Punish loud public moves. Iren is the right first stop. From there, the phase plays.
